===== Page 3 =====
I am afraid that on account of staff changes, sickness, and other calamities, it has not previously been possible to deal with these draft Estimates. However, as there is nothing in particular to be done with them all present, I trust that this delay will not greatly matter.

The despatch promised in the second paragraph of (1) has never come, and it seems likely that the final submission of the Straits Settlements Estimates for the 5/8's approval has been postponed in order to react to both the revenue and expenditure sides of the Budget in the light of more recent developments. This draft was prepared before the war. The question of special formation presence in its disposal has still to be settled, as well as the effect of other conditions on works programmes. In the circumstances, I doubt if I need do much more than tabulate the position on the 26th October, when these estimates were presented.

The final figures for 1930 were:

- Revenue: 69,958,265
- Expenditure: 40,698,115
- Surplus for the year: 29,257,150
- Surplus balances (New a/c): 94,756,532
- Option Revenue Replacement Fund: 57,057,722
- Includes special Defense gift or contribution - $2,000,000
- Takes into account transfer of currency profits - $33,437,129

===== Page 4 =====
The revised estimates for 1939 were:

Revenue: 37,401,306
Empire: 52,675,991
Deficit: 15,274,685
Surplus Balances: 79,500,000
Opium Fund: 57,000,000

(1) Includes 21,000,000 special gift for Imperial Defence.

(2) Reduction reflects fall in value of securities.

The draft estimates for 1940 provided as follows:

Revenue: 37,504,129
Empire:
 - Recurrent: 37,027,777
 - Extraordinary: 8,097,576
Total Empire: 45,200,293
Deficit: 7,696,164

The Revenue estimate was based on conditions ruling in the first half of 1939, and the result of the changed situation with regard to rubber would require an upward revision.

Reductions in Opium Revenue (1 million dollars) and Interest on Investments (600,000 dollars) were estimated for, and were explained by the Financial Secretary on p.3 of the Log Co. debate (end. 4).

Increased expenditure on Police (181,407 dollars) and Medical Services (382,211 dollars, not including PWE) were also planned; otherwise [POSSIBLY MISSING CONTINUATION].

===== Page 5 =====
No important changes seem to have been contemplated. A special item for (civilian) Defence, amounting to about $600,000, has been inserted under Miscellaneous Services, including A.R.P. Careership.

The cash position is summarised on p.4 of the Financial Memo, enclosure 2.

Comment on the general financial position seems superfluous; it would have been sound enough to justify the estimated deficit, but it is quite likely that no deficit on so large a scale will actually be incurred.

It will presumably not be affected by The Gov's address, which has already been summarised in Press telegrams and recorded in the War Budget file. Most of what is in it we know already. I note also the following:

A. More than $4½ million has been invested in food reserves in Malaysia.

B. Jewish refugees. ? Extract to Gen. file.

C. Committee on science teaching appointed.

D. Progress of Fisheries School. ? Extract to Jap. Fishermen file (Secret).

E. Revised plans for development of Singapore General Hospital. ? Extract from the economist on hospital. Health service, war or war. ? Extract to file.

F. Subsidised housing for poorer classes under concern.

It is normal to send copies of.

===== Page 6 =====
The address) at Mr Ridgway, but I am afraid this year they will come too late for anything except reference purposes. I apologize for this.

===== Page 7 =====
The draft Estimates forwarded in 1 have been revised in certain respects. The estimate of revenue has been increased by $699,549 and the estimate of recurrent expenditure by $121,288. On the other hand, extraordinary expenditure has been reduced by $60,310. Thus the budgetary position for 1940 is as follows:

Revenue $38,203,678
Expenditure -
Recurrent 37,224,005
Extraordinary 8,037,266
$45,261,271
Net deficit $7,057,593

Apart from the slump years, 1931 and 1932, the financial record has been one of steady progress, and in each of the years 1933 to 1936 inclusive, the Colony was able to show a surplus on the year's working. The figures of revenue and expenditure from 1937 onwards are as follows:

Years. Revenue. Expenditure. + Surplus. - Deficit.

1937 37,348,383 42,038,482 -4,690,099
1938 69,955,265* 40,698,115 +29,257,150*
1939 40,226,609 45,368,263 -5,141,654
appr. est.
1939 37,401,306 52,675,991 -15,274,685
rev. est.

* includes $33.4 mills. representing currency profits transferred to general revenues.

 includes special gift of 1 million pounds for Imperial Defence.

The fall in the revenue for 1939 is mainly accounted for by reduced receipts from liquor, tobacco and opium ($1.6 mills), dollars, and from the depreciation of investments ($500,000), but the Governor explains that since the Estimates were framed,

===== Page 8 =====
There has been a distinct improvement in the revenue. He expects, therefore, that the official revenue figure for 1939 will be in the neighborhood of $38.6 million, and that expenditure will probably be further reduced from $52.6 million to about $50 million. Thus, the Colony may come out with a deficit of only $11.3 million for 1939, which is perhaps not unsatisfactory, particularly when one remembers the special gift of 1 million pounds (approximately $8.5 million) for Imperial Defence which was paid out of revenues during the year.

The estimated revenue for 1940 is almost identical with the revised estimate for 1939, the figure of $38.2 million being only approximately $600,000 above the 1939 figure.

The estimated expenditure of $45.3 million compares almost exactly with that approved for 1939.

Thus, the Colony has budgeted for a deficit of just over $7 million, which is approximately $2 million more than that originally estimated for 1939, the difference being accounted for by the variation between the revenue estimate of $40.2 million for 1939 and $38.2 million for 1940.

It seems probable that the estimate of revenue will be more than realized—and it will be noted that, even on the present figures, there is a safe margin of approximately $980,000 between the revenue figure and the estimated recurrent expenditure. On the other hand, if we ignore the windfall from the currency fund which put a different complexion on the 1938 budget, it should be noted that there has been a deficit in the accounts only of the past 3 years, and that such a position is not one which can be regarded with complete equanimity. At the moment, there is no undue need for anxiety, and we can, of

===== Page 9 =====
course, rely upon the Government to keep a watchful eye on events as the year proceeds; but it may be a case for the pruning knife in 1941.

Revenue:

The details of the various heads of revenue as compared with the 1939 Estimates are as follows:

Head 1. Port, Wharf, etc. dues.

No change.

Head 2. Licenses, Excise, etc.

$23,454,834 represents a sum about midway between the approved and the revised Estimates for 1939.

The estimated revenue from sales of opium has again been cut by $500,000 to $6.5 million as against the original estimate of $7 million for 1939, although it is to be noted that the revised Estimate for 1939 has only $6 million. Similarly with the figure of revenue from imported liquors, which has been cut by $50,000 to $3.75 million as against the revised Estimate for 1939 of $3.6 million. The tobacco duties are expected to bring in $5.25 million as originally estimated in 1939 although the revised Estimate for 1939 has only just over $5 million.

The only other item which appears to call for comment is the estimate of $6,500 under Item 21 - Licenses for the importation of regulated textiles. The 1938 revenue was $60,185 and the revised Estimate for 1939 is $108,436 as against $55,200 in the approved Estimates. I cannot find any explanation of this cut, but it may be in anticipation of some war restrictions?

Head 3. Head Count.

$2,383,858 as compared with the original Estimate of $2,422,310 and the revised Estimate of $2,229,451 for 1939.

===== Page 10 =====
Item 35. Hire of Police.

This item formerly appeared under the title - Police to private persons, Hire of. The explanation of the rather considerable increase to $2,833 to $117,730 is contained in paragraph 17 of the Governor's despatch.

Head 4. Posts and Telegraphs.

$378,700 as compared with the original estimate of $4,018,500 and the revised estimate of $3,744,000 for 1939. It is anticipated that telegrams and W-T licenses will show an increase; but postal receipts have been marked down $300,000 and fairly substantial cuts have been made in the Estimates under Item 2 - Money Orders and Postal Orders; Item 7 - Services to P.O.s.B. and Item 9 - Telephones.

Head 5. Land on Government Property.

$1,808,317 as compared with the original Estimate of $1,884,827 and the revised Estimate of $1,317,265 for 1939.

Item 4. Government buildings let for profit - $416,770 which is approximately $80,000 less than the approved 1939 Estimate.

Head 6. Interest.

$5,230,605. As might be expected there have been all-round reductions here as compared with the original Estimate of $6,160,689 for 1939:

- Item 10 - Interest on investments - $600,000
- Item 12 - Interest on loans - $75,329
- Item 14 - Interest on purchase money - $249,800

Head 7. Miscellaneous.

$1,478,998 as compared with the original Estimate of $1,317,389 and the revised Estimate of $1,150,957 for 1939. The Colony's share of currency profits is put at $684,500 as compared with $400,000.

===== Page 11 =====
$400,000 in 1939.

Heads 8 and 9. Land Sales and Grant-in-Aid. C.D.F.

No material changes.

Expenditure:

Turning to the other side of the Budget, I have pointed out above that the total expenditure for 1940 is estimated at practically the same figure as that approved for 1939.

I annex a table in which I have attempted to show the variations under the main heads of expenditure as compared with the original as well as the revised Estimates for 1939. Ignoring the gift of $8.5 million for Imperial Defence for which provision was included under Head 37 Miscellaneous in the revised Estimates for 1939, economies amounting to $1,192,272 were effected in the revision of the 1939 Estimates. Column 3 of the table will show the effect which these economies had on the various departmental national budgets.

The provision for 1940 is shown in Column 4 with some explanatory notes in the Remarks Column of the table.

It will be noted that the provision under Public Works Extraordinary has been reduced by over a million dollars and that minor reductions have been made under one or two other heads. On the other side, additional provision of over $600,000 has been included under the Miscellaneous head, the increase being accounted for by enhanced contributions to the Malayan Establishment Office of $243,249, and by the provision of $661,000 included for defence measures, against a paying of $233,668 under the itinerary contribution. On the other hand, the increase to effect all possible economies has enabled the Government to reduce its contributions by $233,668.

The other noteworthy increases occur under the

===== Page 12 =====
The heads Medical (Health Branch; and Hospitals and Dispensaries) and Police, and to a lesser extent under Head 45 - Public Works. The reasons for the increases are fully explained in paragraphs 16 to 18 of the Governor's despatch.

It now remains to set out the position with regard to the Colony's surplus balances. At the end of 1938, the surplus balance (including the windfall from the Currency Fund) stood at $94.75 million. Allowing for a deficit of 11.3 million in 1939, the estimated surplus at the end of 1939 is $83.45 million, which is the equivalent of approximately 2 years' revenue.

The surplus of $83.45 million may be regarded as liquid to the extent of $61.45 million; but against this surplus there are the Colony's commitments and contingent liabilities now reduced to $12.26 million (paragraph 9 of the Governor's despatch) plus the estimated deficit in 1940 of $7 million.

Deducting these amounts, and allowing $5.5 million for Treasury working balances, the liquid balance at the end of 1940 should be $36.69 million; or if allowance is made for the possible depreciation in the market value of the Colony's investments (the Governor suggests a figure of $2 million) at least $34.6 million.

Moreover, it seems from paragraph 11 of the Governor's despatch that there is a sum of $3.1 million, which will accrue to the Colony from the Currency Fund as its share of currency profits and reimbursement of Malayan non-apportionable expenditure for the period ended 31st December 1938. Thus with a surplus balance of $64.19 million, of which $37.7 million is liquid, the position may still be regarded as a strong one.

===== Page 13 =====
I assume that it will not be considered necessary to revert at this time to the question of setting up a Reserve Fund (Mr. Galsworthy's minute of 16.1. on the 1939 draft Estimates - see the last paragraph). We should now approve the Estimates by telegram and, subject to observations C.S.D. may have as regards paragraphs 14 and 15 of the Governor's despatch, confirm the telegram by despatch and approve the creation of the post of Assistant Financial Secretary in Class IV of the Malayan Civil Service.

(continued above)

M. A. Hewlett
27.1.40.

The position is still extremely serious, with an estimated surplus of #83 million in addition to the Reserve Replacement Fund of #57,000,000 (the latter representing approximately nine years of premium revenue at the current rate). It is not clear that the Colonial Government have any long-range guiding force as regards the extent to which the surpluses can be drawn upon to meet annual deficits; but present circumstances are hardly suitable for discussion of such a question at a later time.

===== Page 14 =====
Reserve Fund foreral.

Better revenue and expenditure would be largely affected by the war and the actual alteration are of less than usual value. As regards detail it is unnecessary to add to Greenhill's careful analysis, except to underline the fact that it has been possible to provide for public solicitors and transmission of the social services. In particular, several new appointments for local candidates appear in the Medical and Health offices:

29.1.40

I agree as regards the action proposed.

2. It is curious that, through the Straits dependencies, in a large measure, depend on the general prosperity of the country, which in turn hinges on rubber and tin. There is no reference anywhere to tin and rubber prospects. Remarkably little comment has been made in any of the papers sent as to the revenue estimates; all the attention has been concentrated on the expenditure side.

3. One point may be worth mentioning. In the comparison between recurrent revenue and expenditure, the whole of the "public works-extraordinary" expenditure is omitted. That, of course, gives [POSSIBLY MISSING]

===== Page 16 =====
A distorted view of the position. This head-covering roughly 4½ million dollars is in fact largely recurrent and must be so regarded.

It is no use sticking on a label—"extraordinary"—and taking that as representing the real situation. A glance at the details will amply confirm this view. An administration of this size must have large expenditure under this category; and it matters not at all, from the financial point of view, whether it is classed as extraordinary or not—except that, to a considerable extent, expenditure under this head is much more flexible than under the ordinary "recurrent" head.

3. The budget is, broadly, a "carry-on" budget. It was framed in time of peace; and war problems have not perhaps, even yet, shaped themselves at all clearly. The financial position generally is remarkably sound; and there is little need, therefore, for any comment.

The 30th January, 1940.

The financial strength of the Straits Settlements Government is annually displayed in the Estimates and jealously guarded by a strong Finance Committee of the Legislative Council comprised of substantial men of business who serve as Unofficial Members of the Council.

The general lines of the present Estimates have been clearly and fully set out in the preceding minutes, and there is no cause to be in the least apprehensive.

===== Page 17 =====
Apprehensive over the excess of expenditure over the Revenue Estimates in 1940. The Colony's loan position is sound. It has surplus assets amounting to over two years' revenue, apart from a special opium reserve little short of a similar amount. The circumstances of war, as affecting the Straits Settlements, are likely to strengthen this already sound position.

As regards Sir J. Campbell's minute, it will be appreciated that the year-to-year prosperity of the tin and rubber industries are of much greater concern to the F.M.S. than to the Colony. In the F.M.S. a substantial portion of the revenue is contributed by those industries, and in framing their Estimates that Government has to take a view of the likely range of output and prices which are very materially reflected in public finances. But in the Straits Settlements, although no doubt the general prosperity of Penang and Singapore is ultimately linked with that of the Malay States, revenue does not reflect quick changes in the condition of the tin and rubber industries. The following is an illustration:

| Year | F.M.S. $ million | S.S. $ million |
|------|-----------------|---------------|
| 1926 | 80              | 36            |
| 1927 | 82              | 37            |
| 1928 | 73              | 38            |
| 1929 | 79              | 54            |
| 1930 | 65              | 32            |
| 1931 | 52              | 26            |
| 1932 | 43              | 44            |
| 1933 | 47              | 31            |
| 1934 | 58              | 34            |

1935 [POSSIBLY MISSING]

===== Page 18 =====
It will be seen that whereas the F.M.S. Estimates between 1926 and 1932 dropped from £80,000,000 to £43,000,000, and by 1937 had gone back again to £80,000,000, the Straits Settlements range of revenue has been fairly steady with one or two exceptional years only. As regards Sir John Campbell's second point, the important thing is what he says at A, and in the F.M.S. in particular we have continually had to rely on an ability at any stage in the year to make drastic cuts in extraordinary expenditure.

As the Governor has asked for telegraphic approval of the Estimates, it would be desirable to telegraph simple approval without delay and follow up by a despatch on the lines proposed by Mr. Greenhill and Mr. Martin; after which the Medical Adviser should see the Governor's despatch with its observations regarding the new buildings for the hospitals and the College of Medicine, and Mr. Cox should see the reference in paragraph 16 of No. 2 to the incidence of leprosy amongst school children and the investigation which is being instituted in that connection.

30.1.40.

The Colonial Secretary, Straits Settlements, presents his compliments to the Under Secretary of State for the Colonies and has the honour to forward herewith Ordinance No. 57 of 1941, entitled "An Ordinance for making Final Provision for the Public Service for the year 1940" together with the Attorney-General's certificate and report on the Ordinance.

2. The usual certificate of authentication has been signed by the Clerk of Councils and is kept for record here.

===== Page 23 =====
I am of opinion that the Governor's assent may properly be given to a Bill entitled "An Ordinance for making Final Provision for the Public Service for the year 1940.".

Attorney-General's Chambers,
Singapore, 11th November, 1941.

===== Page 24 =====
Enclosure No. 3 to Straits Despatch

24 December 1911

===== Page 25 =====
The expenditure for the year 1940 on certain of the items detailed in the Supply Ordinance, 1939, exceeded the provision therein made to the total extent of $17,344,873.80. This Ordinance gives legislative sanction to such extra expenditure.

The Schedule to this Ordinance details only the net excesses of the various items in the Schedule to the Supply Ordinance, 1939. On other Heads of the latter Schedule, savings to the amount of $3,234,806.45 were made.

Attorney-General,
Straits Settlements.

ATTORNEY-GENERAL'S CHAMBERS,
SINGAPORE, 11th November, 1941.

===== Page 26 =====
No. 57 of 1941.
I assent,

T. S. W. THOMAS,
Governor and Commander-in-Chief.
27th November, 1941.

An Ordinance for making Final Provision for the Public Service for the year 1940.

[5th December, 1941].

WHEREAS by the Supply Ordinance, 1939, a sum not exceeding forty-one million two hundred and thirty-eight thousand nine hundred and seventy-six dollars was charged upon the revenues and other funds of this Colony for the service of the year 1940, in addition to the sums to which such revenues and other funds were liable under the provisions of the Widows' and Orphans' Pension Ordinance and the Contribution to Imperial Defence Ordinance, to be appropriated for each service as detailed in the Supply Ordinance, 1939.

And whereas the expenditure in respect of certain of such services during the year 1940 exceeded the amount so charged upon the revenues of this Colony by seventeen million three hundred and forty-four thousand eight hundred and seventy-three dollars and eighty cents in the proportions as hereinafter specified: It is hereby enacted by the Governor of the Straits Settlements with the advice and consent of the Legislative Council thereof as follows:

1. This Ordinance may be cited as the Final Supply Ordinance, 1941.

2. The further sum of seventeen million three hundred and forty-four thousand eight hundred and seventy-three dollars and eighty cents shall be charged

===== Page 27 =====
Upon the revenues and other funds of this Colony for certain Public Services for the year 1940 to be appropriated in the proportions detailed in the Schedule hereto annexed.

SCHEDULE.

3. Charitable Allowances ... 198.69
4. Governor ... 1,168.78
8. Colonial Secretary, Resident Councillors and Resident ... 31,041.84
9. Secretary to High Commissioner ... 18,811.07

21. Estate Duty Office ... 26,731.66

   III Local Forces ... 813,840.23
   IV Emergency Measures ... 32,069.85

50. Transport ... 655.76
52. Veterinary ... 10,597.31

Passed this 5th day of November, 1941.

H. P. BRYSON,
Clerk of Councils.

An Ordinance for making provision for the Public Service for the year 1940.

[29th December, 1939].

WHEREAS the expenditure for the service of the Government of the Straits Settlements for the year 1940 has been estimated at the sum of forty-one million two hundred and thirty-eight thousand nine hundred and seventy-six dollars:

It is hereby enacted by the Governor of the Straits Settlements with the advice and consent of the Legislative Council thereof as follows:

1. This Ordinance may be cited as the Supply Ordinance, 1939.

2. A sum not exceeding forty-one million two hundred and thirty-eight thousand nine hundred and seventy-six dollars shall be and the same is hereby charged upon the revenues and other funds of this Colony for the service of the year 1940 in addition to the sums to which such revenues and other funds are liable under the provisions of the Widows' and Orphans' Pension Ordinance and the Contribution to Imperial Defence Ordinance; and the said sums so charged may be expended as hereinafter specified, that is to say:

1. Charge on account of the Public Debt ...
2. Pensions, Retired Allowances, Gratuities, etc. ... ... 2,758,276

===== Page 45 =====
1. With reference to my Straits Settlements Despatch No. 354 dated the 8th November, 1939, on the subject of the Estimates of Revenue and Expenditure of this Colony for 1940, I have the honour to forward herewith the following documents:

Encl: (1) (a) a copy of each of the Reports of the Singapore, Penang, and Malacca Select Committees on the draft 1940 Estimates;

Encl: (2) a copy of the Report of the Joint Select Committee;

Encl: (3) a copy of the Proceedings of the Legislative Council of the 8th November, 1939, relative to these Reports and Estimates; and

Encl: (4) a copy of the Estimates of Revenue and Expenditure for 1940 as passed by the Legislative Council. Twelve additional copies of each of these enclosures are being sent by ocean mail.

2. The Supply Bill to give legal authority to the approved Estimates of Expenditure was introduced by the Financial Secretary at the meeting of the Legislative Council on the 11th December, 1939, when it was taken through all its stages and passed.

3. For the general financial position of the Colony I would refer you to the memorandum on the draft Estimates forwarded with my Despatch quoted above.

The Right Honourable
Malcolm MacDonald, M.P.,
etc., etc., etc.,
COLONIAL OFFICE.

===== Page 46 =====
4. The 1940 Estimates now provide for revenue of $38,203,678, expenditure of $45,261,271, and an anticipated deficit of $7,057,593.

5. Since the Estimates were framed there has been a distinct improvement in the revenue, and if the improvement is maintained, the figure of $38.2 million for 1940 will certainly be an under-estimate. The 1939 revenue, the revised estimate of which is given in the memorandum on the draft Estimates referred to in paragraph 3 above as barely $37.5 million, now appears from the most recent figures to be likely to reach $38.6 million. The drop on the original 1939 estimate will thus be $1.6 million, and is mainly accounted for by reduced receipts from liquor, tobacco, opium, and interest on investments.

6. The estimated expenditure of $45,261,271 for 1940, which includes revotes under Public Works Extraordinary amounting to over $1 million, is nearly $107,000 less than the original estimate for 1939. The individual heads of expenditure are dealt with in the memorandum on the draft Estimates.

7. The anticipated deficit of $7,057,593 for 1940 is $1,915,939 more than the original estimated deficit for 1939. This increase is accounted for by the difference between the revenue estimates — $40.2 million for 1939 and $38.2 million for 1940.

8. The deficit on the current year's working, originally estimated at $5.1 million, was revised in the memorandum on the draft 1940 Estimates to $15.25 million, and the increase was attributed to the drop in revenue and to the gift of 31 million to the Imperial Government. But as stated in paragraph 5 above, it now appears that the drop in revenue will be only $1.6 million instead of $2.8 million, while on the other hand, the revised estimate of 1939 expenditure can probably be reduced from the $52.75 million shown in the memorandum to $50 million.

===== Page 47 =====
The latest estimate of the deficit on the 1939 working is therefore $11.3 million.

9. The Colony's surplus balance at the close of 1938 was $94.75 million. At the end of 1939 it should, on the above calculation, be approximately $83.45 million.

Against that surplus, apart from the estimated deficit of $7.1 million for 1940, there are shown at page 3 of the Estimates total commitments and contingent liabilities of $12.26 million. These commitments show a decrease of over $7.5 million on the 1939 figures, chiefly owing to the progress of the Singapore Town Improvement Scheme, to the payment of another instalment of $2 million toward the Special Defence Contribution, and to the cessation of the Singapore Harbour Board's borrowings in respect of the West Wharf Extension loan.

10. The surplus of $83.45 million may be regarded as liquid to the extent of $61.45 million. After deducting $5.5 million as the amount required for Treasury working balances and contingencies, the estimated deficit for 1940 of $7.1 million, $12.26 million for commitments and liabilities, and $2 million for possible depreciation in the market value of the Colony's investments, the available liquid balance on the 31st December, 1940, should be about $34.6 million.

11. The foregoing takes no account of the sum of $3.1 million which will accrue to the Colony from the Currency Fund as its share of currency profits and reimbursement of Malayan non-apportionable expenditure for the period ended the 31st December, 1938.

12. In May of this year, in view of the increasing expenditure on Civil Defence measures necessitated by the international outlook, Heads of Departments were instructed to revise their budget proposals for 1940 and to observe the strictest economy consistent with the maintenance of normal progress.

===== Page 48 =====
Progress and development. On the outbreak of war, further steps were taken to ensure that the proposals for next year were limited to the essential needs of the Colony, but no attempt was made to interfere with proposals for the development of social services. It will be found, in particular, that the provision for Education and Medical and Health Services shows an increase on the current year's estimates.

13. I enclose a schedule setting out the more important additions to the Estimates under Personal Emoluments and giving references to the relevant correspondence.

14. Under Malayan Civil Service, Class I B, there has been inserted "additional provision" for the officer who is performing the duties of Secretary for Defence.

15. It will be seen that the post of Stamp Officer and Assistant Treasurer, Singapore, is no longer shown under Class IV, Malayan Civil Service. This post was created at the beginning of 1938, but there was no officer available to fill it until May 1938, by which time the financial reorganization had taken place. The Treasurer and Deputy Treasurer had become the Financial Secretary and Deputy Financial Secretary in the Colonial Secretariat, and the Treasury was under the Accountant-General, a professional officer. The first available officer was accordingly gazetted as Stamp Officer only, and was employed part-time in the Stamp Office and part-time assisting the Deputy Financial Secretary in the routine business of the Secretariat and of the Currency Commissioners. It had been evident for some time that more assistance was needed in the Financial Secretary's branch, so when a suitably qualified Straits Settlements Civil Service officer became available in July 1939, he was appointed Deputy Commissioner of Stamps, and the Malayan Civil Service officer was free to devote the whole of his time to the Financial Secretary's branch. The appointment is now shown in the Estimates as Assistant Financial Secretary, and I consider it important that

===== Page 49 =====
The post should continue to be filled by an officer of Class IV of the Malayan Civil Service in order to provide real assistance to the Financial Secretary and Deputy Financial Secretary. The post will serve as a most useful training ground for suitable officers who may later be appointed to more responsible financial posts.

16. Further increases have been made in the staff of the Medical Department, largely owing to the continued expansion of the hospital services to which I refer below in connection with Public Works Extraordinary, and to the steady increase in the population of the Mental Hospital, Singapore, which has risen from 1,079 in 1930 to 1,504 in 1938, and to 1,725 in 1939.

Extra staff has also been provided in the Health Branch, together with the necessary office, ward, and laboratory accommodation, for work which I regard as of the greatest importance - the diagnosis and treatment of leprosy in school children. The matter, which is complementary to the more careful examination of immigrants for leprosy instituted a year ago, has been under investigation for some time. Inspections of school children in Singapore revealed that approximately one per cent showed signs of early leprosy. The Unofficial Members of Legislative Council in Joint Select Committee readily agreed to the provision required in the firm conviction that in the years to come this expenditure will be more than balanced by a saving on the segregation and treatment of adult lepers. I share their conviction and intend to do all I can to further this work.

17. It will be seen that there is also a considerable increase in the Police Force personnel. In my Straits Settlements Despatch dated 19th May, 1939, I have already addressed you regarding new appointments for senior Police officers. The number of

===== Page 50 =====
Asiatic Inspectors grow steadily in accordance with the policy of replacing European Inspectors by Asiatics. In the subordinate ranks, there is an increase of nearly 300 men in the Malay, Indian, and Chinese contingents, to meet the ever-increasing calls on the Police, particularly in connection with the development of new areas and the growth of population. In addition, nearly 200 Sikhs are being recruited to provide guards for the Royal Air Force aerodromes, and their cost will be recovered from the Royal Air Force.

18. The major items under Public Works Extraordinary appearing in the Estimates for the first time are as follows:

(a) General Hospital, Singapore
Page 142, item 11, Quarters for Nurses 50,000 371,000
- 142, - 12, New Medical Wards and Teaching Clinic 5,000 405,000

(b) College of Medicine, Singapore.
Page 142, item 17, Students' Hostel 1,000 499,000
- 142, - 18, New Buildings for the Department of Anatomy 25,000 165,000

(c) Medical, Province Wellesley.
Page 146, item 18, Relief Hospital, Butterworth 50,000 79,000

All the above items were considered by an Advisory Committee on major public works consisting of the Financial Secretary, the Director of Public Works and the Unofficial Members of Legislative Council, before they were passed for entry in the draft Budget.

(a) The items for the General Hospital, Singapore, are part of an extended programme which calls for explanation.

It had been clear for some time past that the provision of hospital accommodation in Singapore was not adequate.

===== Page 51 =====
To meet the demands of a growing population of the labouring classes, and that a rearrangement was necessary to enable the students of the College of Medicine to be given the maximum advantage of the material available to advance their training, I accordingly appointed an Informal Committee consisting of the Financial Secretary and three Unofficial Members of Legislative Council to consider the question of hospital services in Singapore and suggest a policy. The Committee's report, which has been accepted in principle by my Executive Council, recommends certain extensions to the existing General Hospital which comprise a programme spreading over a number of years. The new buildings required, in their order of priority, are:

(1) Staff Quarters, two blocks - 1939
(2) New Operating Theatre for the Teaching Surgical Unit in the Third Class Wards - 1939
(3) Medical Wards and Teaching Clinic - 1940
(4) First block of 200 beds for Children - 1940
(5) Special department and First Class Medical Ward
(6) First Class Maternity block to be converted into a Children's Infectious Diseases block
(7) New Maternity block for First Class patients.

Item (1) is provided for in item 10 on page 142 of the 1940 Estimates, which is an expansion of item 2 on page 161 of the 1939 Estimates in order to provide for two blocks instead of one.

Item (2) was provided for by Special Warrant ($81,000) in August this year, and the work is progressing satisfactorily.

Item (3) is entered in the 1940 Estimates as stated above.

Item (4) has had to be postponed on account of difficulty with the site and pressure of work in the Public Works Department, but will be proceeded with as rapidly as possible.

===== Page 52 =====
Items 5 to 7 are not regarded as urgent but will be put into effect in due course.

The above programme entails a steady and considerable increase in the Hospital staff, and accordingly new quarters are required for both Sisters and Nurses. As they are required more urgently for the Sisters, the former item "New Nurses Quarters", which first appeared in the 1937 Estimates, has been altered to "New Sisters' Quarters" (page 142, item 9, 1940 Estimates) and the above-mentioned new item "Quarters for Nurses" has been entered.

There may be some delay if the war interferes with the supply of building materials but, fortunately, no question of finance need hamper this comprehensive programme for the extension and modernization of the Singapore General Hospital.

(b) The new Students' Hostel is necessitated by the demolition of an old building which housed some of the students, and by the increasing numbers who are being admitted to the College from the 1st June, 1939.

The new buildings for the Anatomy Department at the College are also necessitated by the increase in students.

(c) The Relief Hospital at Butterworth is the first half of a project intended to deal with chronic cases from the General Hospital and the District Hospitals in the Penang Settlement, with the object eventually of segregating cases of chronic tuberculosis.

19. I should be obliged if your approval of these Estimates could be conveyed to me by telegram.

I have the honour to be,

Sir,

Your most obedient, humble servant,

GOVERNOR.

===== Page 86 =====
Extract from the proceedings of a meeting of the Legislative Council of the Straits Settlements at Singapore on Wednesday, the 8th November, 1939.

MOTION BY THE FINANCIAL SECRETARY.

The Financial Secretary: Sir, I move that this Council adopts the recommendations respecting the Estimates for 1940 made by the Select Committee for Singapore and Labuan in Council Paper No. 99 of 1939, by the Select Committee for Penang in Council Paper No. 100 of 1939, and by the Select Committee for Malacca in Council Paper No. 101 of 1939, as amended by the joint report of these three Committees sitting together which has been laid on the table today as Council Paper No. 102 of 1939.

The Revenue Estimates have been revised and increased by $699,549. Recurrent Expenditure is up by $121,288, but this is practically balanced by a contra revenue entry under Class II.

In dealing with Expenditure Extraordinary, the Select Committee has aimed at closer budgeting and the elimination of works which could conveniently be postponed without detriment to normal development. In the result, there is a reduction of $1,371,000, but against this must be set the foreseen revotes which have come to hand since the Draft Estimates were compiled and certain other necessary additions, so that the net decrease in Expenditure Extraordinary is $60,310.

The final result is that the estimated deficit has been reduced from $7,696,164 to $7,057,593.

I should like, Sir, to express my thanks to the Unofficial Members for the time and care which they have devoted to the task of scrutinising the Estimates.

Sir, I move the motion standing in my name, No. 2 in the Orders of the Day.

===== Page 87 =====
The Colonial Secretary:— Sir, I rise to second the motion.

The motion was agreed to.

MOTION BY THE FINANCIAL SECRETARY.

The Financial Secretary:— Sir, I move that this Council approves the Draft Estimates of Revenue and Expenditure for the year 1940.

This motion has already been proposed and seconded at the last meeting of this Council. The Draft Estimates have now been amended and stand amended as in the Resolution which has just been passed.

Sir, I move the motion standing in my name, No. 3 in the Orders of the Day.

The Colonial Secretary:— Sir, I rise to second the motion.

The motion was agreed to.

EXPENDITURE

NOTE:—The Offices printed in italics are non-pensionable.

* Denotes “Change in amount due wholly or in part to the operation of an authorized Scheme of Salaries.”

† “Previous provision insufficient.”

‡ “Previous provision excessive, or no longer required.”

Where incremental rates of salaries are shown they are printed thus:—The first group of figures represents the initial salary; the next group the amount of the increment, followed by a letter denoting the length of the incremental period; and the last group of figures represents the maximum salary. The letters denoting the incremental periods are:—A.=annual; B.=biennial; T.=triennial. [Thus $6,300–300A–7,200 denotes a salary of $6,300 rising to $7,200 by annual increments of $300.]

Sir,

I have the honour to forward herewith the following documents in connection with the Estimates of Revenue and Expenditure of this Colony for the year 1940:

Encl: (1). (a) A copy of the draft Estimates of Revenue and Expenditure for 1940;

Encl: (2). (b) A copy of a Memorandum by the Financial Secretary on the draft 1940 Estimates compared with the 1939 Estimates;

Encl: (3). (c) A copy of my Address to the Legislative Council on the 16th October, 1939; and

Encl: (4). (d) A copy of the verbatim report of the debate on the budget.

Twelve additional copies of each of these enclosures are being sent with the duplicate of this despatch by ocean mail.

2. A further despatch will be addressed to you, dealing with the later stages of the proceedings, when the Estimates have been passed on the 8th November.

I have the honour to be,

Sir,

Your most obedient, humble servant,

GOVERNOR.

The Right Honourable
Malcolm MacDonald, M.P.,
etc., etc., etc.,
COLONIAL OFFICE.

===== Page 316 =====
STRAITS SETTLEMENTS

Paper to be laid before the Legislative Council by Command of His Excellency the Governor

Memorandum on the Draft 1940 Estimates of Revenue and Expenditure, compared with the 1939 Estimates

FINANCIAL POSITION—GENERAL

On the 1st January, 1939, the Colony’s General Revenue Balance amounted to $94.75 millions, of which approximately $75.6 millions were liquid. The budget for the current year showed an estimated expenditure of $45,368,263 and an estimated revenue of $49,226,609. The estimated deficit was therefore $5,141,654. It is now anticipated that the revenue for 1939 will barely reach $37.5 millions but, on the other hand, owing largely to the gift of £1 million to the Imperial Government, the revised estimate of expenditure indicates an increase to about $52.75 millions.

The deficit on the year’s working should thus be about $15.25 millions, and the General Revenue Balance on 31st December, 1939 should be $79.5 millions.

The following statement sets out the resultant position as regards the Assets and Liabilities of the Colony at the end of the current year, compared with the actual position on the 1st January, 1939.

===== Page 319 =====
The estimated revenue for 1940 is $37,504,129, which is $2,722,480 less than the original estimate for this year and $102,823 more than the revised estimate.

The total estimated expenditure for next year amounts to $45,200, a figure which is $167,970 less than the original estimate for the present year and $7,475,698 less than the revised estimate.

There is thus an estimated excess of expenditure over revenue of $7,696,164.

The following items of 1940 estimated revenue show a marked contrast to the original estimate for 1939:

Class I, Duties, Taxes and Licences:

The Opium Revenue shows a decrease of $1,000,000, and the Liquor Revenue and Registration of Trade Marks decreases of $50,000 each.

The Licences for the Importation of Regulated Textiles show a decrease of $48,700, and Stamp Duties (various Revenue services) show a decrease of $200,000.

Class II, Fees of Court or Office, etc.:

Specialists Fees amounting to $150,000 are now shown under Hospitals Board, "Hospital Bills recoverable".

Class III, Posts and Telegraphs:

There is a decrease of $300,000 under "Postal Receipts".

Class IV, Rents on Government Property:

Government Buildings let for profit show a decrease of $79,510.

Interest:

Interest on Investments shows a decrease of $600,000. Interest on Loans and Advances shows a decrease of $75,329. Interest on Opium Purchase Money Outstanding shows a decrease of $249,800.

Class V, Miscellaneous Receipts:

Share of Currency Profits shows an increase of $284,500.

Overpayments recovered show a decrease of $145,440.

To forecast the cash position of the Colony it is necessary to take into account the estimated deficit on next year's working and also the known commitments. The commitments consist of money which the Colony has undertaken to lend to the Municipality, Malacca, amounting to $25,000, undertakings under the Heads Drainage and Irrigation, Defence Contribution Special, and Public Works Extraordinary estimated to cost nearly $6.3 millions, and contingent liabilities in respect of grants to the Singapore Improvement Trust, Penang Municipality, and Rural Board Province Wellesley totalling $3.9 millions. In all, these commitments aggregate to about $10.2 millions, but though the whole of this amount is taken into calculation it is unlikely that more than $1 million will be required in 1940.

The estimated General Revenue Balance on 31st December, 1939, is nearly $79.5 millions, of which approximately $56.6 millions can be regarded as liquid. To ascertain the estimated cash surplus available, a sum of $5.5 millions which is required for Treasury working balances and for a reserve against contingencies must be deducted from the liquid portion of the surplus, leaving $51.1 millions.

===== Page 320 =====
The estimated 1940 deficit of $7,696,164 and the commitments totaling about $10.2 million are a charge against the liquid surplus. Hence, the estimated balance of cash available at the end of 1940 will amount to about $33.2 million.

At the end of 1939, the Opium Revenue Replacement Reserve Fund is estimated to stand at $57 million. The interest earned by the Fund in 1940, estimated at $2.4 million, will be credited to Revenue and is included in the Revenue Estimates.

EXPENDITURE

1. Charge on account of the Public Debt

No provision is required in 1940. The interest and sinking fund in respect of the 3% Loan 1962–72 are payable by the Singapore and Penang Harbour Boards.

2. Pensions, Retired Allowances, Gratuities, etc.

Increase: $56,416

Increased provision is required for “Superannuation Allowances,” “Police Pensions,” “Pensions of Widows and Orphans,” and “Refund of Bachelors’ Contributions under the Widows’ and Orphans’ Pension Ordinance.”

3. Charitable Allowances

There is no change in the total amount under this head. The Vote “After-care Association, S.” is increased by $300, but there is a reduction of $300 in the Vote “Relief of Distressed Chinese Women, S.”

4. Governor

Personal Emoluments: Increase $748
Other Charges, Annually Recurrent: Decrease $1,480
Other Charges, Special Expenditure: Increase $6,130
Net Increase: $5,398

Personal Emoluments.—The increase is due to increments under approved schemes and to revision of salary of the post of “Senior Stenographer and Confidential Clerk.”

Other Charges, Annually Recurrent.—The decrease is due to reductions in the Votes “Transport and Travelling Allowances” by $1,000 and “Miscellaneous” by $400 in the light of the actual expenditure in recent years.

The decrease of $80 in the Vote “Telephone Subscriptions” results from the lowering of the Telephone Company’s rates.

Other Charges, Special Expenditure.—The increase is due to provision for the purchase of a motor-car to replace an old car which it is uneconomical to maintain.

There is no change in the total amount under this head. 16. Customs and Excise

Personal Emoluments ... Decrease $511
Other Charges, Annually Recurrent ... Decrease $25,565
Other Charges, Special Expenditure ... Decrease $45,865
Net Decrease $71,941

Personal Emoluments.—The decrease is due to changes of holders which account for a reduction of $12,581, offset by normal increments which account for an increase of $12,070.

Other Charges, Annually Recurrent.—The decrease is due to:

(a) the effort made to exercise the maximum economy in all items of expenditure and to provide for essential services only;

(b) the improved system of converting raw opium into prepared opium which has resulted in the closing down of one boiling room at the Opium Factory with consequent savings in expenditure on fuel and food;

(c) the closing down of five Retail Chandu Shops owing to the decreased sales of prepared opium.

Other Charges, Special Expenditure.—The decrease is due to:

(a) the transfer of the Vote “Textiles Quotas Administration” to Other Charges, Annually Recurrent;

(b) there being no provision for any large items of expenditure compared with 1939;

(c) curtailment of repairs and other expenditure at the Opium Packing Plant to what is absolutely essential.

25. Immigration and Passports

Fees collected under the Aliens Ordinance (Chapter 90) are paid into the Aliens Immigration Fund. Expenditure under Personal Emoluments is recoverable from this Fund, but expenditure under Other Charges, appearing in Appendix I, is paid direct from the Fund. Revenue from Passport fees is credited to the Fund as a reimbursement for the cost of collecting and protecting Passport Revenue.

Under this Head are shown only Personal Emoluments of officers engaged directly by this Department. The seconded officers, viz., two Malayan Civil Service officers and nine clerks from the General Clerical Service, are shown under other Heads and all the expenditure is recoverable from the Immigration Fund. The estimated Revenue and Expenditure of the Fund for 1940 is shown in Appendix I.

Many of the text books and other works frequently used are out of date and it is considered advisable to replace them by the latest editions of such works.

37. Miscellaneous Services

Personal Emoluments ... Increase $168,150
Other Charges, Annually Recurrent ... Increase $151,384
Other Charges, Special Expenditure ... Increase $166,862

Total Increase $486,396

Personal Emoluments.—

(a) “Contribution to Malayan Establishment Office” accounts for an increase of $147,437, due to the raising of the rate of contribution from 27% to 30%. (This is an advance payment, subject to adjustment).

(b) The item “Confidential Assistant to Defence Committee, Malaya—$3,892” has been transferred from the head “Colonial Secretary, Resident Councillors and Resident”; the officer is under the Colonial Secretary for administrative purposes but is not a member of the Colonial Secretary’s Office staff.

(c) The items under “Malayan Currency Commission” totalling $25,716 were formerly shown under “Treasury”. Under the Currency Ordinance, 1938, the Board of Commissioners of Currency, Malaya, controls the currency, both notes and coin, and the Currency Commissioners’ staff has no longer any connection with the Colonial Treasury. The whole of this expenditure is recoverable from the Currency Commissioners.

(d) “Housing Allowances” are reduced by $5,500 because certain charges hitherto paid from this vote will in future be paid from “Transfer and Miscellaneous Travelling Allowances” under the head “Transport”.

(e) There is a reduction of $4,000 on “Allowance on Pensions”.

===== Page 348 =====
37. Miscellaneous Services—continued

Other Charges, Annually Recurrent.

(a) “Contribution to Rural Boards” is increased by $9,827, the principal factor being an estimated fall in revenue at Malacca of $13,155, due almost entirely to estimated lower receipts from Motor-car Taxes and Registration of Bicycles.

(b) “Contribution to League of Nations Health Organisation Eastern Bureau” is increased by $4,000. This is a Malayan contribution, of which only the Colony share ($3,000) was hitherto shown in the Colony Estimates. It has now been agreed that the whole Malayan contribution, which is recoverable from the Currency Surplus, shall for the sake of convenience be included in the Colony Estimates.

(c) The item “International Malaria Course $1,500”, was hitherto shown under Medical, Special Expenditure. The League of Nations is expected to contribute $1,000, and half the balance is recoverable from the Federated Malay States.

(d) “Contribution to Malayan Establishment Office” is increased by $95,812, due to the raising of the rate from 8% to 10%. (This is an advance payment, subject to adjustment).

(e) “Contribution under Section 23 of the Singapore Improvement Ordinance” is increased by $45,000. This contribution represents the cost of Town Improvement schemes approved by Government.

(f) “Reimbursement to Singapore Harbour Board in respect of Sunday Working”—an increase of $3,000. This is dependent on the state of trade, and may prove an underestimate in view of the recent increases in the tin and rubber quotas.

Other Charges, Special Expenditure.—Under this sub-head have been entered fifteen items, totalling $661,900, to provide for Defence measures necessitated by the war. The estimates had to be made within a month of the outbreak of hostilities, in several cases before the scope of the emergency measures and the staffs required to work them were known, and accuracy cannot be claimed for these figures. Some of this expenditure will be recovered from other Administrations, but except for that in connection with the Secretary for Defence, Malaya, the apportionment has not yet been settled.

Provision of $10,000 is made for preliminary work in connection with the 1941 Census.

There is a decrease of $233,638 in the Contribution to Rural Boards, due chiefly to the cutting down of Public Works Expenditure in response to the call for economy which was made before the outbreak of war.

Normal increments under approved salary schemes account for $1,942.

The post of Office Assistant has been abolished on the retirement of the holder of the appointment, with a resultant decrease of $3,900.

Other Charges, Annually Recurrent.—The decrease is mainly due to the reduction of $4,000 in the vote “Extra Labour” consequent upon the above-mentioned additions to the permanent staff, and to the deletion of “Commission on Sale of Publications.” Government has opened its own Publications Bureau and commission is no longer paid.

Other Charges, Special Expenditure.—The total of $9,690 includes $3,000 for A.R.P. equipment, as the Printing Office is regarded as one of the essential services, and compares with the 1939 total of $19,842, which included a second Monotype Caster to cope with the increasing statistical work.
