===== Page 362 =====
207

===== Page 363 =====
Honourable Members of the Legislative Council,

I propose, as last year, to read only certain portions of my Address, leaving you to study the rest at leisure. When I addressed you at the Budget Meeting of last year, I was able to give expression to the relief which we all felt at being spared the horrors of war and the hope that we might be blessed with real and enduring peace. That hope has not been fulfilled. Great Britain is once again at war, fighting for the freedom of the world from Nazi domination. In this, she has the full support of the British Empire which, as in 1914, has placed its whole resources at the disposal of the Mother Country in her hour of need. We know that victory will be ours, and we can only pray that the sacrifices that are being demanded of our people and of France, that great champion of liberty, will bring lasting happiness to the world.

Here in Malaya, our duty is clear. Last year this country set an example to the Colonial Empire in its contributions to Imperial Defence. The Colony of the Straits Settlements alone made a free-will offering of £1 million and decided also to increase its annual contribution by $2 million for a period of five years. Most of the Malay States gave according to their means, and I felt very proud that I should be the channel through which these voluntary tokens of loyalty were communicated to the Secretary of State. I have no doubt that in the coming months, further examples of this spirit of generosity will be forthcoming; but I wish to remind you that war is a hard taskmaster and that victory cannot be won unless each individual person gives of his or her best. It is not only of the men in the fighting line that sacrifices will be demanded; it is from each one of us here in Malaya. In times like these, it becomes necessary to impose restrictions on normal business and other activities. We have, for instance, had to take control of certain imports and exports. I wish to assure you that in so doing we are fully conscious of the necessity for avoiding so far as

===== Page 364 =====
It is possible that any harmful interference with the trade of this Colony, which is of vital importance to the Empire, could have serious consequences. We have also had to control the prices of food and other essential articles, as well as rents. We have demonstrated our intention to deal drastically with profiteers. Whatever anyone of us may think, we are all similarly placed, and the fortunes of all of us stand or fall together. Individually we may not be able to do much, but collectively we can do a great deal: by being yet more zealous in our work, by accepting quietly such decisions as may be imposed in the common interest, by refraining from making difficulties and from spreading rumours.

In a letter which I received a few days ago from a correspondent, he spoke of the need for "quiet efficiency," and I can think of no better words. Anyone who, in these days, by careless talk or to suit personal inclinations, throws sand into the wheels so that the machinery which has been set up runs less smoothly is doing harm to the State and thereby to the cause for which the Empire is fighting.

I know that this is generally recognised, but I refer to the subject now because I feel that the inability to realise the real horrors of war, thanks to the quiet security in which we live here, may as time goes on become a danger and give rise to boredom and perhaps friction. That is something against which we must all be on the watch, and if I may presume to offer advice, I would suggest that in all our daily avocations we should apply the simple test of comparing our lot with that of those men who are fighting for us in Europe, and of the people in England and France, who are submitting cheerfully to perils, restrictions, and hardships of which no one who has not actually experienced them can have any conception.

In other words, let us continue to put our

===== Page 365 =====
Country first and ourselves afterwards.

We have been fortunate in having had time to prepare against an emergency. The crisis of September 1938 was of immense value in that it showed up the weak spots in the various schemes for civil defence, and I desire to express my sincere gratitude to all those, whether members of the fighting Services or of the civil Government or of the public, who have spent so much time and trouble during the past year in working out detailed plans. They have given of their best, and in most cases the work was additional to their normal duties.

It has been possible during the past year to increase very substantially the garrison of Malaya. We have been privileged to welcome a number of squadrons of the Royal Air Force with fast and powerful machines, and reinforcements of British and Indian soldiers. Having regard to the long association of this country with India, it is peculiarly appropriate that in this time of stress we should receive aid from that great Empire and that included in the garrison there should now be units of Indian troops whose record is a proof of their efficiency and fighting qualities. We are grateful to them for their presence here, and they have the admiration of us all.

I have referred on other occasions to the volunteers and I need only repeat that the Government will always be ready to consider cases of hardship that may arise out of the war. I wish here also to remind you that one of our most important duties during the past few weeks has been to keep the public fully informed of the actions taken by the Government and to explain the reasons so far as possible. A Department of Information has been set up which has already done much good.

===== Page 366 =====
Work is being constantly developed. A system of public broadcasts has been arranged by means of loudspeakers placed in the most thickly populated areas in Singapore. We intend to do the same in the other Settlements, and for rural districts, travelling vans with loudspeakers are being equipped. Regarding more extensive broadcasting, I am able to say that a service is about to be established that will leave no room for criticism. We have had to wait long for advice, but we have now received it, and the necessary apparatus has already been ordered.

All these war measures cost money. For example, the Governments of the Straits Settlements and the Federated Malay States have already jointly advanced rather more than $2.1 million to be invested in Malayan food reserves, and arrangements have been made, or are being considered, for further substantial purchases. We hope to recover most, if not all, of these sums, and as real expenditure, a better example is the vote for Air Raid Precaution which to date amounts to $400,000. Yet, the desire and intention of this Government is that measures already taken or projected for the development of social services and public health shall not be curtailed. It will be realized, of course, that our resources must be devoted primarily to the successful prosecution of the war. But our financial position is fortunately such that, while giving adequate aid in this way, we can from our surplus balances carry out a reasonable programme of progress and development. That shall be our aim.

The budget which is presented to you today was framed in time of peace and as such it will require the most careful examination. The closing of the accounts for the year 1938 showed an actual surplus of $29.2 million against an estimated deficit of $2.3 million. This was rendered possible.

===== Page 367 =====
By the transfer of $33.4 million from the Currency Fund soon after the coming into force of the new Currency Ordinance. Without this addition, the actual deficit would have been over $4 million, half of which is accounted for by the gift of $\frac{1}{2}$ million to the Imperial Government for defence purposes, and by the first instalment of $1\frac{1}{2}$ million of a Special Defence Contribution totalling $10 million to be spread over five years. Also, $\frac{1}{2}$ million was voted to the Penang and Province Wellesley Silver Jubilee Fund, over $900,000 to the Singapore Improvement Trust, and $895,000 was spent on purchases of land, which included a site for the new St. Andrew's School and a site at Haig Road on which subordinates' quarters are now being built. When allowance is made for these unprovided items, it will be seen that the ordinary expenditure was well within the revenue.

The revised estimate of revenue for 1939 is $37.4 million and the revised estimate of expenditure is $52.7 million, so that on present figures the close of the year should show a deficit of $15.3 million. This is $10.2 million more than was anticipated at the beginning of this year, and the deficit is largely accounted for by the contribution of one million pounds towards Imperial Defence. The other principal items of expenditure for which provision was not made originally are $329,484 for the Singapore Improvement Trust and $265,000 for Geylang Reclamation, items which reflect the Government's determination to continue the work of town improvement steadily and uninterruptedly.

The A. R. P. Vote of $50,000 had, as I have already said, to be extended by $350,000 to meet the urgent necessity of accelerating the precautionary measures as the international situation became worse. I take this opportunity to express my high appreciation of the manner in which people of all races and all classes

===== Page 368 =====
Have come forward to co-operate in this work.

For 1940, the revenue is estimated at $37.5 million. This estimate was made before the outbreak of war. The recurrent expenditure is estimated at $37.1 million, or only $400,000 less than the estimated revenue. There is an increase of $4 million over the current year, chiefly due to increase of staff in the Police and Medical Departments: in the former due largely to the outbreak of war, and in the latter due to the expansion of hospital and health services on which, war or no war, we do not propose to economise.

Over $8 million had been entered for Special and Extraordinary Expenditure before war broke out and further examination of the budget may make it possible to reduce this. But, in the exercise of economy we must endeavour to avoid creating unemployment, and this should not be very difficult, for although there are some Departments which cannot run at full speed in time of war, several new offices have been called into existence by the war itself and require considerable staffs.

In the Public Works programme, the principal new works concern the Medical Department to which I refer later. The need for each of these works is great and I am of the opinion that their commencement should not be postponed: in these difficult times the rate of progress may be slower than usual, but that is no reason why they should not be commenced.

The general revenue balance at the end of 1939 is estimated at nearly $79.5 million, with another $57 million in the Opium Revenue Replacement Reserve Fund.

I am glad to say that in the Colony the year under review has been peaceful and quiet. The spread of war in China and the military occupation of large

===== Page 369 =====
Parts of that country from which so many of our Chinese immigrants come have caused grave concern to our Chinese population, but once again I am able to pay tribute to the restraint which they have shown. Some uneasiness was shown following the publication of the Anglo-Japanese declaration on the 24th of July and, as Honourable Members will remember, I referred to this at the meeting of this Council on the 31st of July. Fortunately, the Chinese in this country showed their commonsense in refusing to be stamped by hasty conclusions.

There has been no change in the position of Indian labour. In spite of the severe restriction on rubber production during the first part of the year, it was found possible to provide work for any labourers who might otherwise have been thrown out of employment. For this purpose, full use was made of the permission granted to estates to carry large stocks of rubber and also of the considerable amount of re-planting, new planting, and more intensive cultivation which was taking place generally.

As you are aware, the position of Indian labourers in this country is now under discussion with the Government of India. In an attempt to reach a settlement, a delegation was sent to Delhi at the beginning of the year under the leadership of Mr. C. D. Ahearne, former Federal Secretary, Federated Malay States. The points at issue between the two Governments were clarified, but at the moment I can only say that further correspondence has taken place and I sincerely hope that an agreement satisfactory to all parties will result.

As regards Chinese labour, there was a certain amount of unemployment in Malacca, which was tackled by arranging for relief work to be undertaken.

===== Page 370 =====
By the Drainage and Irrigation Department. The male and female immigration quotas have remained at 500 per month each since April and May 1938, respectively. There have been no major strikes, though in the building trade there was a little difficulty in April and May. Building workers have frequently suffered loss of wages through the default of sub-contractors, this being their most serious cause for discontent. An agreement has been reached whereby in effect the principal contractors guarantee wages for half a month and the workers themselves are enabled to keep a much closer watch on the activities of the sub-contractors.

LABOUR LEGISLATION.

Bills have recently been published for general information dealing with the settlement of industrial disputes and the registration of trade unions, and they should eventually have a far-reaching effect on local conditions of labour.

JEWISH REFUGEES.

Between October 1938 and March 1939, just over 100 Jewish refugees were permitted to land in Penang and Singapore. The majority were refugees from Germany and suitable work has been found for them in Malaya.

EDUCATION.

I turn now to education. The Report of the Commission under the Chairmanship of Sir William McLean, to whose appointment I referred in my Address last year, is being printed in London and we are awaiting the receipt of copies for distribution.

In regard to Science, a Malayan Committee has been appointed "to advise regarding the aims and general nature of the science teaching in English schools in the Straits Settlements and Federated Malay States and the type of laboratories and equipment required for that teaching".

An Educational Conference was held in Kuala Lumpur in August 1939. It was attended by delegates from the Straits Settlements, Federated Malay States.

===== Page 371 =====
and Unfederated Malay States; and there were fruitful discussions on educational questions in general and the problems connected with English, Malay, Chinese, and Indian schools in particular. A report of the proceedings will be issued.

The Church of England Zenana Mission School for Girls, Singapore, hitherto unaided, became a grant-in-aid English school with effect from the 1st of January, 1939. This is the oldest girls' school in Malaya and since its foundation in 1833 has rendered notable service to education.

The progress of vocational education referred to last year has continued, and at the Singapore Trade School 114 pupils are enrolled, of whom 69 are taking the course in general mechanics, 16 the electrical, 12 the radio engineering, and 17 the domestic engineering course. Twenty-nine successfully completed the three-year course, and all found employment.

The Penang Trade School has an enrolment of 151, of whom 98 are taking general mechanics, 30 electrical engineering, and 23 domestic engineering course. Eleven successfully completed the three-year course, and all found employment.

The Malacca Trade School has an enrolment of 51; 23 are being instructed in carpentry and 28 in tailoring. Eighteen successfully completed the three-year course, and ten have found employment.

St. Joseph's Trade School, Singapore, opened by the Gabrielist Brothers, a Canadian Order with experience of similar undertakings elsewhere, has been given a capital grant towards the purchase of equipment and has been promised a grant-in-aid for two years.

The enrolment in Government and aided English Schools is 27,279 as compared with 26,560 in 1938. In

===== Page 372 =====
Private English schools number 10,393 as compared with 10,013 in 1938. In addition, there are 1,284 pupils in the Singapore Government afternoon English classes as compared with 1,146 in 1938.

Teachers for the middle and upper classes of English schools are normally selected from graduates of Raffles College who have taken a fourth year course in Education. However, it is not yet possible to get a supply of women graduates for Penang and Malacca, where elementary Normal Class three-year courses for women have had to be formed.

There are 173 Malay boys' schools and 48 Malay girls' schools with 26,842 pupils as compared with 25,714 in 1938. Most of the schools are in rural areas, and a syllabus suited to the environment of the pupils is followed with emphasis upon gardening and handwork, physical training, games, and hygiene.

The Malay Women's Training College at Malacca is now in its fourth year and is rapidly becoming a force in women's education. Twenty-four students who had successfully completed the two-year course, three of them gaining first class certificates, were absorbed into the schools at the end of 1938.

Grants-in-aid are also provided for Chinese and Indian schools, the amounts paid last year being $82,210 for Chinese schools and $20,755 for Indian schools. The total number of pupils in all Chinese schools is 50,271 and in all Indian schools 3,365.

Special attention continues to be paid to the health and physical welfare of pupils. Particularly noticeable is the result of propaganda among the girls, and there has been marked improvement in the physique and bearing of the Malay girls as a result of physical training. All the Government and aided English schools for girls have had school sports this year, and the Chinese schools are equally progressive.

===== Page 373 =====
The Fisheries School at Singapore is now ready and equipped for its duties. The school will endeavor to provide instruction in more modern methods of fishing, and it is hoped that eventually an industry will be built up which will be capable of satisfying the whole of Malaya's needs for fresh fish. A full fishing unit consisting of power-craft and four auxiliary boats has been provided. There is a complete set of nets of various kinds, and spare parts are available for instruction in making and mending and for actual work at sea. A course of navigation is included in the curriculum and will enable the fishermen, when trained, to journey to more distant fishing grounds.

Despite the fact that the school is essentially for training Malays, demonstrations and instruction will be made available for any section of the fishing community at convenient times. It is expected that improved methods of salting and drying of fish can be demonstrated and instruction given in the art of canning and even cooking fish from time to time.

Two important new laws affecting children were brought into force during the year: the Adoption of Children Ordinance, which gives statutory recognition to the position of adopted children as is now granted by the statute law of England, and the Children Ordinance, which is the outcome of the recommendations of the Minority Report of the Commission appointed to investigate the question of mui-tsai and transference of women and children in Hong Kong and Malaya. This re-enacts the general provisions of the Children

===== Page 374 =====
Ordinance includes a new part governing the notification of girls transferred from their parents' custody, subject to certain exceptions, and the protection of those transferred girls who are in need of it.

The public health of the Colony has been good. There has been no outbreak of infectious disease, and the incidence of malaria shows a marked decrease compared with the previous year, with Singapore having been almost completely free. Strict anti-malaria control has been maintained near garrison buildings, and measures taken by the Medical Department to protect troops recently arrived from India have been highly successful.

Rural hygiene work is yielding gratifying results.

In my Address last year, I outlined the proposed scheme for enlarging the General Hospital, Singapore. The scheme has been modified, and plans are now being prepared for:

- A new operating theatre connected to the surgical clinic and surgical outpatient department, opened in the old dental clinic;
- A new block containing the medical clinic for the College of Medicine, currently at Tan Tock Seng Hospital, and providing 160 beds. This block will also contain the medical outpatient department;
- A new block for special diseases: ophthalmic, and ear, nose, and throat. This block will include special outpatient clinics for these diseases and, on the top floor, new first-class wards for medical cases;
- A new block to accommodate nursing sisters;
- An additional block to accommodate [POSSIBLY MISSING]


===== Page 375 =====
Extra nurses for the new wards;

A new children's hospital which will eventually provide accommodation for 400 children by including the present maternity wards. At some future date, a new small maternity block will have to be built for first class patients only;

Accommodation for additional staff.

At Kandang Kerbau Maternity Hospital, Singapore, the second block of buildings which contains ante and post natal clinics and up-to-date labour wards is now nearly complete.

In Penang, the site for the new maternity hospital on the old race course is being prepared.

The medical arrangements at the three civil airports in Singapore and Penang now conform with the International Sanitary (Air Navigation) Convention.

A most valuable and interesting report on leprosy in the Colony has been rendered by Dr. G.A. Ryrie, Medical Superintendent at Sungei Buloh. This disease has cost the Government large sums of money annually and has been the special care of the Medical Department for the past two years. I hope that the measures which have been taken will cause leprosy gradually to disappear. Immigrants are now examined for very early signs of the disease and a number of cases have been detected and returned to the port of departure. Doctors are being trained to detect early leprosy in school children who, it has been found, are responsive to treatment and can be cured. The Director of Medical Services has been instructed to prepare a statement of the financial provision necessary to carry out this scheme of inspection which will, if possible, be extended to include the general health of school children. A scheme

===== Page 376 =====
For the erection of a leper settlement for females north of Butterworth, Province Wellesley, has been approved. The present female leper camp at Jelutong, Penang, will be closed and the patients removed to the new settlement by the sea where they will be housed in healthy and happy surroundings.

The installation of the new water filtration plant now gives Malacca an excellent water supply. The construction of the Cherok Tok Kun dam and impounding reservoir in Province Wellesley has been completed. The reservoir provides sufficient storage in conjunction with the existing impounding reservoir at Berapit to supply Butterworth, Prai, and Bukit Mertajam.

The Committee appointed to review the Singapore Improvement Ordinance and to make recommendations for its amendment have completed their work and a new Bill is being drafted.

Arising out of one of the recommendations of this Committee, the question of subsidised housing for the poorer classes of the community has been engaging the attention of Government but will require further consideration in the light of the new situation created by the war.

The work of the St. John Ambulance Association has made rapid strides since the beginning of the year. The September crisis of 1938 inspired the public to learn first aid and home nursing and to join the Brigade.

Lectures and subsequent examinations have been undertaken by doctors and trained sisters on an entirely voluntary basis, and warm thanks are due to them for their readiness to help without which it would have been impossible to carry out the teachings of the Association. The training

===== Page 377 =====
Is given to persons of all nationalities, and where possible, arrangements are made for instruction in Chinese and Malay. The brigade plays an important part in the Air Raid Precautions scheme of defence for the Colony and will be responsible for the manning of all first aid posts in an emergency. The Association is the only form of auxiliary nursing service in Malaya in time of war; women, especially, are urged to take the course in first aid and home nursing and then join the St. John Ambulance Brigade, which is a uniformed body. Members should keep themselves efficient in order to assist the professional nursing staff of Malaya when required.

The activities of the Drainage and Irrigation Department in Malacca are expanding rapidly into all parts of the Settlement, instead of being concentrated mainly in the Central District. During the past four years the Malacca river has been dredged and 4,000 acres of rice land have been drained. 1,750 acres near Jasin have been irrigated, and the Duyong drainage scheme, for the reclamation of 2,000 acres of swamp and the improvement of 1,600 acres of existing rice land, has just been completed. Much of the land in this important area, close to Malacca town, has only gone out of cultivation during the past ten years, and there are already encouraging signs of re-colonization.

In Province Wellesley the Sungoi Kulim scheme will irrigate 4,500 acres, and it is hoped to extend this scheme so as to include an extra 2,500 acres. A programme for the removal of silt from rivers has been commenced, and work

===== Page 378 =====
On the Muda bund, which will protect the northern part of the district from floods, progress is being made. When these are completed, valuable areas now waterlogged will be restored to fertility.

The rice crop for the 1938-39 season, estimated at 24,500,000 gantangs, shows an increase of 2 million over the previous season. I need hardly emphasise how important it is for this country to increase its own production of essential food supplies, and Honourable Members can rest assured that the Government, for its part, will do all in its power to encourage and facilitate any feasible projects to that end.

Honourable Members, in this time of stress and trial, I am happy to be able to rely on your loyalty and devotion to the cause of the Empire. In that faith, therefore, I pray that you may be guided in your deliberations with wisdom and power so that they may prove fruitful in the service of our King and of his subjects in this Colony.

===== Page 379 =====
STRAITS SETTLEMENTS

LEGISLATIVE COUNCIL.

SINGAPORE, MONDAY, 16TH OCTOBER, 1939.

10.30 a.m.

===== Page 380 =====
Extract from the proceedings of a meeting of the Legislative Council of the Straits Settlements at Singapore on Monday, the 16th October, 1939.

MOTION BY THE FINANCIAL SECRETARY.

The Financial Secretary:—Sir, I move that this Council approves the Draft Estimates of Revenue and Expenditure for the year 1940.

The draft Budget was in print when war broke out and it was not practicable to scrap the fruits of months of laborious work and produce in a few weeks a new set of figures drawn up in the light of very altered circumstances. The Budget, indeed, reflects a certain degree of economy which was insisted upon in view of the expenditure to which the Colony was being put in regard to precautionary measures and the accumulation of reserve stocks of various kinds, but one must be prepared to find that the result of next year's working will tell a somewhat different story from the draft estimates which are being presented today.

Before proceeding further, I ought to say a word as to the method adopted in compiling the estimated figures of revenue. They are based very largely on actual receipts for the 12 months immediately preceding the month in which they are drawn up. In framing the revenue figures for next year the Treasury has, therefore, taken as its guide the returns over a period disturbed by political storms which hampered business and impeded enterprise. The feeling of nervousness, if not of pessimism, affecting as it did the trade and commerce of the Colony has been reflected in the public revenue, and on the figures available today the estimate of revenue for the current year is not likely to be realised.

It follows too from what I have said regarding the method of estimating that the anticipated revenue of $37,504,129 for next year, which is $2,722,480 less than the original estimate for the present year, has been framed on none too optimistic lines, and had we been in a position to look forward to a year of normal trading I should not have hesitated to say that the estimate of revenue is a conservative one. I can, however, say this: I feel no doubt that whatever sums may be required to meet the abnormal conditions created by the war should be found without much difficulty.

I should also like to add a general remark about the expenditure estimates. All proposals for next year's Budget were subjected to the closest scrutiny but, in spite of this, there may be items which in the existing circumstances will not be regarded as justifiable. I have in fact already consulted my colleague, the Director of Public Works, in the matter and his Department has offered certain suggestions which will be placed before the Honourable the Unofficial Members in their proper time. But perhaps I ought to say now that it is not proposed to take any action or effect any economies which will tend to retard the normal progress and development of social service.

In view of what I have said I do not think that Honourable Members will wish me to take up their time in dilating on figures which at best are little more than an attempt to present a picture drawn on orthodox lines of a year's working under normal conditions. I shall, therefore, confine my remarks to matters which in any event would have called for some comment.

===== Page 381 =====
On the Revenue side, there is a drop of over $1,300,000 under Licences, Excise and Internal Revenue, as compared with the current year's estimate. This is mainly accounted for by an estimated decrease of $1,000,000 in the Opium Revenue. As Honourable Members know, this is the result of a policy adopted by the Colony to reduce progressively the sale and consumption of opium.

The estimated decrease of $300,000 in Postal Receipts is largely due to a change in the method of accounting, and there has been a consequential reduction in the vote "Conveyance of Mails," so that the drop is more apparent than real.

The item Interest on Investments shows a drop of $600,000 as compared with the estimate for the present year. When addressing this house at the Budget Meeting last year, I said that interest is a form of revenue which is largely fortuitous since it depends so much on the size of the Colony's investments. Owing to the unfavourable world conditions which have persisted for some time past, the gilt-edged market was adversely affected, and as the situation grew more critical, prices of securities pursued a downward trend. There have been demands on the Colony's surplus funds, notably the Special Defence Contribution, and the 3,000,000 gift to the Imperial Government. Anticipating that there might be some large calls on the surplus funds, I thought it prudent in the circumstances to retain a substantial sum in the Joint Colonial Fund instead of investing it. This Fund is administered by the Crown Agents for the Colonies and is made up of deposits from the various Colonies and Dependencies. For some time past, the Fund has consistently paid an annual dividend of two per cent. This is, of course, less than the return from gilt-edged investments but there is no risk of capital depreciation and I think that we were fortunate to have been in a position to make these very large payments without incurring a loss which would have been inevitable had it been necessary to realise investments.

Another cause for the drop in interest is the fact that the Colony's surplus has been reduced by the payments to which I have just referred.

So much for revenue.

On the other side of the Budget, Recurrent Expenditure is estimated at $37,102,717, and Expenditure Extraordinary at $8,097,576. The former shows an increase of $758,724 over the present year's estimate which can mainly be accounted for by essential increases in the Police personnel, the development of medical services and the contribution to the Malayan Establishment Office. Expenditure Extraordinary shows a decrease of $926,694 on the present year's figure, and as I have already hinted, there will probably be further reductions before the Budget is finally submitted to this house.

Before concluding, I should like, Sir, to mention a sub-head which makes its appearance in the Budget for the first time. I refer to "Defence," which is shown under Miscellaneous Services, Special Expenditure. It relates to the cost of the civil side of the organisation required to meet the present state of emergency. There is one item under this special head, namely, "Foreign Exchange Control," which is of particular interest to the business community of the Colony. As Honourable Members know, the currency of this country is based on a sterling exchange standard. Hence, it follows that any control imposed in the United Kingdom must, to be fully effective, have its counterpart here. It was, of course, necessary to adapt the Home Regulations to the special circumstances of the Colony and in doing so every effort has been made to avoid undue interference with

===== Page 382 =====
Legitimate trade while at the same time ensuring the requisite measure of control over the export of currency. In working out a practical plan suitable to the peculiar needs of this country and in ironing out the creases which made their appearance in the initial stages, I have had the willing co-operation of the banking and commercial community and I gladly acknowledge my indebtedness to them.

Sir, I move the motion standing in my name, No. 6 in the Orders of the Day. (Applause).

The Colonial Secretary: — Sir, I rise to second the motion.

Mr. Khoo Sian Ewe: — Sir, in 1937 Government was good enough to establish a farm school at the Agricultural Demonstration Station, Ayer Itam, Penang. This school has now been in existence for nearly three years and has made satisfactory progress. The students who have passed out will, I am sure, be able to raise the standard of agricultural practice in Penang and on the mainland. I understand that most of these students come from poor families and I hope that, after the completion of their course, Government will afford them the opportunity of putting into practical use the teaching imparted to them. I therefore suggest, Sir, that arrangements might be made to assist such students as may require assistance by the grant of a few acres each of suitable Crown land on favourable terms. This will not only enable them to earn their living, but will also encourage parents to send their boys to the farm school. This proposal, if adopted, will stimulate the production of agricultural products at little cost to Government and, at a time like the present when every effort should be made to encourage food production, I hope, Sir, that Government will give to my proposal the sympathy it deserves.

I also wish to refer to a matter concerning education in Penang, that is the teaching of languages in the St. George's Girls' School. I am informed that no facilities are provided for the teaching of languages in this school, as a result of which many of the senior girls had to seek admission into aided schools where such facilities exist. As the knowledge of at least one foreign language is necessary to enable students to take up higher education in our colleges and universities, I hope Government will make the necessary provision for the teaching of languages in the above school. (Applause).

Mr. G. Wiseman: — Sir, I do not intend to take up much time of the Council but as it is a time for economy I would suggest that great economies could be made in some branches of the Government's building operations. As a member of the Rural Board of Malacca I have often criticised the, to me, high cost of erection of Government coolie lines. In this year's estimates there is a six-roomed concrete coolie line to be erected at Selandar in Malacca territory to cost $5,300 or $883 per room. I mention this item as on an estate that I am connected with in Johore similar accommodation is being erected at a total cost of $1,800 or $300 per room; this figure includes preparation of site, cement drains, etc. These permanent lines that I am referring to are passed by the Controller of Labour and meet Government requirements. It is possible that the Government coolie lines are more elaborate than the planters' but is this necessary? The duty of scrutinising Public Works expenditure lies upon the Unofficial Members of Council; in many cases of proposed expenditure which are put up to them the Unofficial Members are unable to bring to bear that degree of technical knowledge which should be applied if any really intelligent analysis of the matter is to be made. I suggest, therefore, that whenever they consider it proper Unofficial Members should be allowed to call in the services of an

===== Page 383 =====
Independent expert whether Architect, Surveyor or Engineer to enable them to deal with such cases more effectively. (Applause).

Mr. C. R. Cherry:—Sir, I would like to take this opportunity of paying tribute to the smooth and efficient manner in which this country was transformed from a peace to a war-time basis on and immediately after the outbreak of hostilities in Europe on 3rd September. To those of us who can recall what happened in August 1914 and compare conditions then with those which obtained on the 3rd of last month, the difference is remarkable indeed. On the commencement of hostilities in August 1914, the trade of Malaya to all intents and purposes ceased for a time. A moratorium was declared and it was not possible to carry on normal business until the banks re-opened their doors and for some while thereafter. In marked contrast to the events of August 1914, trade from 3rd September onwards has continued uninterrupted, if on a limited scale to start with. The banks have remained open and, after a brief interval in which to adjust itself, the commerce of this country has proceeded, except for certain regulations, on very nearly normal lines, and today the volume of business is, I believe, greater than for some time past.

The amount of thought and energy expended by Government in creating an organisation which put into operation so promptly and efficiently the measures necessary for carrying on trade under war conditions must have been great. But the efforts and the work entailed are appreciated by all those who have come in contact with the Government’s activities in this direction. It will have been a source of considerable gratification to Your Excellency that your labours and those of your officers have resulted in such a satisfactory state of affairs, and in this regard I wish to offer congratulations on behalf of the Unofficial Members of this Council. I would ask that our expression of appreciation be suitably recorded. (Applause).

Capt. N. M. Hashim:—Sir, I rise to support the Honourable the Unofficial Member for Singapore, and I convey the thanks of the public, particularly the smaller people, for the prompt and effective action taken by Government in the control of foodstuffs. This prompt action has saved the public from being unduly and unnecessarily imposed upon. It may be said that it is premature to express public appreciation but when credit is due it must be recorded without delay or hesitation. The public fully realise and appreciate the amount of additional work and time put in by Government officials and officers, and particularly by Your Excellency in order to achieve this happy and peaceful state of affairs. The public are grateful.

In spite of the hard times everyone in this country is happy and enjoying peace and security and is going about his usual daily work as he would during normal times and as he would if we were not at war.

The feelings of peace and security are increasing day by day following upon the increase in strength and efficiency of the defence of the country on land, at sea, and in the air.

I am to assure Government that the public are in all respects and at all times with the Government. (Applause).

Now, Sir, I will proceed to the subject about which I gave notice in August. I wish to refer very briefly to the inspection and control of Malay schools. I am very doubtful whether the discipline and general control in these schools are as good as they should be. I do not wish to be misunderstood. I am making no reflection whatsoever on the

===== Page 384 =====
European or Malay Inspectors. The former are, I know, particularly in Singapore, carrying a very heavy burden of work, while the latter as yet lack the experience that is necessary. Until we have a Malay Inspecting Staff with the necessary background and experience, I would urge that the European inspecting staff should be strengthened as soon as time becomes normal again; but in the meantime something should be done to make up for the defect to some extent. May I also suggest, in order to improve the quality and status of the Malay Inspectors, that in future they should be recruited from graduates of Raffles College. It is of vital importance to the future of the Malays that discipline and all that is involved in the formation of good character and right habits should receive special attention in the schools. Speaking as one who has a very intimate acquaintance with the Malay schools, I have to confess very serious doubts as to whether enough is being done in the way of supervision and control to ensure that we are preparing the pupils of today to be worthy citizens of tomorrow. This view has been expressed on several occasions and in connection with several incidents by a good number of responsible Malays both in Singapore and Penang. (Applause).

The Governor:—The points raised by the Unofficial speakers will be dealt with on the motion for the adjournment.

But I should like now to say a few words in reply to the references made by the Honourable the Elected Member for Singapore, and the Honourable the Malay Member, to the work that has been carried out since the outbreak of war.

In my address I expressed my thanks to all those who had spent so much time and labour on working out schemes for defence. The Honourable Members who have spoken have carried this story a little further and they have in the most generous terms expressed the appreciation of the public of the smooth and efficient way in which this Colony passed from a peace to a war footing. I am particularly glad to have this opportunity of speaking on the subject because I have felt, in the absence of public criticism, justified in believing that on the whole things have been going reasonably well, and I was not sure how I could find the opportunity to express my great sense of indebtedness to all those who have carried on the work of their predecessors and who have administered the schemes which have been prepared with so much labour.

In that work of administration the members of the fighting services, members of Government Departments and members of the public have all co-operated fully and wholeheartedly, and I hope that I may here express my personal gratitude to all of them—to the members of the Services from His Excellency the General Officer Commanding downwards; to the officers of Government whether they have had to undertake the administration of a war department or perhaps have had the less interesting but nevertheless equally important work of maintaining quiet and ease in their districts, and also to all those unofficials who have so readily given of their time in helping to maintain efficiency in the Colony during a time of war. It is to all of them of whatever rank, of whatever calling, that any thanks are due for such success as has been achieved in the last six weeks, and I desire to say that I am extremely grateful. (Applause).

The Financial Secretary:—Sir, I now move that the motion be adjourned until the next meeting of Council, and that the Draft Estimates be referred to a Select Committee of Council to sit in three parts—one part of the Select Committee for Singapore and Labuan to consist of the Financial

===== Page 385 =====
Secretary and all the Singapore Unofficial Members of Council; the second part of the Select Committee for Penang to consist of the Resident Councillor, Penang, and all the Penang Unofficial Members of Council; and the third part of the Select Committee for Malacca to consist of the Resident Councillor, Malacca, and the Malacca unofficial member of this Council.

Mr. J. R. Bennett:—Sir, I rise to second the motion.

The motion is agreed to.

COLONIAL ESTIMATES OF THE REVENUE AND EXPENDITURE OF THE STRAITS SETTLEMENTS GOVERNMENT FOR THE YEAR 1940

ORDINANCE NO. OF 1939

REVENUE .................................................. $37,504,129
EXPENDITURE RECURRENT ......................... $37,102,717
SURPLUS .................................................... $401,412

EXPENDITURE

NOTE: The Offices printed in italics are non-pensionable.

* Denotes “Change in amount due wholly or in part to the operation of an authorized Scheme of Salaries.”

† Denotes “Previous provision insufficient.”

‡ Denotes “Previous provision excessive, or no longer required.”

Where incremental rates of salaries are shown, they are printed thus: The first group of figures represents the initial salary; the next group the amount of the increment, followed by a letter denoting the length of the incremental period; and the last group of figures represents the maximum salary. The letters denoting the incremental periods are: A = annual; B = biennial; T = triennial. 

[Thus $6,300–300A–7,200 denotes a salary of $6,300 rising to $7,200 by annual increments of $300.]

10. Rebuilding Bridge No. 4/23 or Balik Pulau—Sungei Pinang Road
